message.content:
THE JUDGE'S BIASES AND THE SCORING MATH — what beats a 49.98 reference?

Files: /home/polaris/wt/flywheel/third_party/deep_research_bench/prompt/score_prompt_en.py, /home/polaris/wt/flywheel/third_party/deep_research_bench/utils/score_calculator.py, /home/polaris/wt/flywheel/third_party/deep_research_bench/deepresearch_bench_race.py.
Also read the DeepResearch Bench paper (arXiv 2506.11763) on RACE's design and validation.

Work out the ARITHMETIC of winning:
- The reference (gemini-2.5-pro-DR) scores 49.98 overall on this board — essentially parity with itself.
- SOTA (cellcog-max) = 55.78. On the dimensions: comprehensiveness 56.34, insight 57.08, IF 55.30, readability 51.94.
- POLARIS = 43.82 (comp 45.49, insight 42.38, IF 44.09, read 37.74) on task 72.
Given Overall = target/(target+reference): what RAW criterion scores must we hit to reach 50 / 52 / 55?
How much raw-score improvement does one point of overall cost? WHERE is the cheapest point on the board for us
(our readability is 37.74 vs SOTA's 51.94 — is readability the cheapest, given it is only 0.14 weight)?
Do the actual maths and show it.

Then search for published work on LLM-JUDGE BIASES relevant here: length bias, structure/formatting bias, position
bias, self-preference, verbosity bias, list bias. Which are DOCUMENTED with numbers, and which apply to a
reference-based pairwise judge like RACE? We need to know how much of the 12-point gap is presentation.

- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.

- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).

3	def calculate_weighted_scores(llm_output_json, criteria_data, language="en"):
4	    """
5	    Calculates weighted scores based on LLM output and criteria weights.
6	    
7	    Args:
8	        llm_output_json: JSON output from LLM with scoring data
9	        criteria_data: Criteria configuration with dimension weights
10	        language: Language of the evaluation (default: "en")
11	        
12	    Returns:
13	        Dictionary with weighted scores for target and reference models
14	    """
15	    results = {
16	        "target": {"dims": {}, "total": 0.0},
17	        "reference": {"dims": {}, "total": 0.0}
18	    }
19	    total_target_score = 0.0
20	    total_reference_score = 0.0
21	    
22	    # Get dimension weights
23	    dimension_weights = criteria_data.get("dimension_weight", {})
24	    task_id = criteria_data.get("id", "Unknown") 
25	    
26	    # Check if criterions exist
27	    if "criterions" not in criteria_data or not criteria_data["criterions"]:
28	        error_msg = f"ID: {task_id} - Missing required criterions data, cannot calculate weighted scores"
29	        logging.error(error_msg)
30	        raise ValueError(error_msg)
31	    
32	    # Create a mapping from criterion text to weight for easier lookup
33	    criterion_weights = {}
34	    for dim, criterions in criteria_data.get("criterions", {}).items():
35	        criterion_weights[dim] = {crit['criterion']: crit['weight'] for crit in criterions}
36	
37	    # Record all unmatched criteria for warnings
38	    unmatched_criteria = set()
39	    
40	    for dim, scores_list in llm_output_json.items():
41	        if not isinstance(scores_list, list):
42	            logging.warning(f"ID: {task_id} - Dimension '{dim}' in LLM output is not a list. Skipping.")
43	            continue
44	
45	        if dim not in dimension_weights:
46	            logging.warning(f"ID: {task_id} - Dimension '{dim}' from LLM output not found in criteria dimension weights. Skipping dimension.")
47	            continue
48	            
49	        if dim not in criterion_weights:
50	            logging.warning(f"ID: {task_id} - Dimension '{dim}' from LLM output not found in criteria details. Skipping dimension.")
51	            continue
52	
53	        dim_target_weighted_sum = 0.0
54	        dim_reference_weighted_sum = 0.0
55	        dim_total_weight = 0.0
56	
57	        dim_criteria_map = criterion_weights.get(dim, {})
58	        
59	        # Skip dimension if no criteria mapping exists
60	        if not dim_criteria_map:
61	            logging.warning(f"ID: {task_id} - No criteria mapping found for dimension '{dim}'. Skipping dimension.")
62	            continue
63	
64	        for score_item in scores_list:
65	            if not isinstance(score_item, dict):
66	                logging.warning(f"ID: {task_id} - Item in scores_list for dimension '{dim}' is not a dictionary. Skipping item: {score_item}")
67	                continue
68	
69	            criterion_text_raw = score_item.get("criterion")
70	            criterion_text = criterion_text_raw.strip() if isinstance(criterion_text_raw, str) else None
71	
72	            # Check different score field formats
73	            # 1. Comparative scoring mode: article_1_score and article_2_score
74	            # 2. Single scoring mode: target_score
75	            article_1_score_raw = score_item.get("article_1_score")
76	            article_2_score_raw = score_item.get("article_2_score")
77	            target_score_raw = score_item.get("target_score")  # Single scoring mode
78	
79	            # If target_score exists but article_1_score doesn't, assign target_score to article_1_score
80	            if target_score_raw is not None and article_1_score_raw is None:
81	                article_1_score_raw = target_score_raw
82	                # In single scoring mode, article_2_score is not needed
83	
84	            try:
85	                article_1_score = float(article_1_score_raw) if article_1_score_raw is not None else None
86	                article_2_score = float(article_2_score_raw) if article_2_score_raw is not None else None
87	            except (ValueError, TypeError):
88	                logging.warning(f"ID: {task_id} - Invalid score format for criterion '{criterion_text}' in dimension '{dim}'. Scores: art1='{article_1_score_raw}', art2='{article_2_score_raw}'. Skipping criterion.")
89	                continue
90	
91	            # If criterion_text exists and article_1_score is not None
92	            if criterion_text and article_1_score is not None:
93	                # Check for exact match
94	                weight = dim_criteria_map.get(criterion_text)
95	                
96	                # If exact match not found, try fuzzy matching
97	                if weight is None:
98	                    # First try case-insensitive matching
99	                    criterion_lower = criterion_text.lower()
100	                    for key, val in dim_criteria_map.items():
101	                        if key.lower() == criterion_lower:
102	                            weight = val
103	                            break
104	                    
105	                    # If still not found, try substring matching
106	                    if weight is None:
107	                        for key, val in dim_criteria_map.items():
108	                            # Check if criterion text contains criteria or criteria contains criterion text
109	                            if criterion_lower in key.lower() or key.lower() in criterion_lower:
110	                                weight = val
111	                                break
112	                
113	                # If still no match found, record and use average weight
114	                if weight is None:
115	                    unmatched_criteria.add(f"{dim}:{criterion_text}")
116	                    # Calculate average weight for this dimension's criteria
117	                    weight = sum(dim_criteria_map.values()) / len(dim_criteria_map)
118	                    
119	                dim_target_weighted_sum += article_1_score * weight
120	                dim_total_weight += weight
121	                
122	                # Only calculate reference part if article_2_score exists
123	                if article_2_score is not None:
124	                    dim_reference_weighted_sum += article_2_score * weight
125	            else:
126	                if criterion_text:
127	                    if dim not in getattr(calculate_weighted_scores, '_warned_dims', set()):
128	                        logging.warning(f"ID: {task_id} - Criterion text mismatch for dimension '{dim}': '{criterion_text}'. Available criteria keys start with: {list(dim_criteria_map.keys())[:1] if dim_criteria_map else []}... Check prompt/output/criteria file consistency. Further mismatches in this dim won't be logged fully.")
129	                        if not hasattr(calculate_weighted_scores, '_warned_dims'): calculate_weighted_scores._warned_dims = set()
130	                        calculate_weighted_scores._warned_dims.add(dim)
131	                    else:
132	                        logging.debug(f"ID: {task_id} - Another criterion mismatch for dimension '{dim}': '{criterion_text}'")
133	                elif not criterion_text:
134	                    logging.warning(f"ID: {task_id} - Missing 'criterion' key in score item for dimension '{dim}': {score_item}. Skipping item.")
135	
136	        if dim_total_weight > 0:
137	            dim_target_avg = dim_target_weighted_sum / dim_total_weight
138	            # Only calculate reference average if reference scores exist
139	            dim_reference_avg = dim_reference_weighted_sum / dim_total_weight if article_2_score is not None else 0
140	        else:
141	            dim_target_avg = 0
142	            dim_reference_avg = 0
143	            if len(scores_list) > 0:
144	                logging.warning(f"ID: {task_id} - No valid criteria scored for dimension '{dim}' despite {len(scores_list)} items in LLM output. Check for systematic mismatches. Dimension average set to 0.")
145	
146	        results["target"]["dims"][f"{dim}_weighted_avg"] = dim_target_avg
147	        results["reference"]["dims"][f"{dim}_weighted_avg"] = dim_reference_avg
148	
149	        dim_weight = dimension_weights.get(dim, 0)
150	        total_target_score += dim_target_avg * dim_weight
151	        total_reference_score += dim_reference_avg * dim_weight
152	
153	    # Log warning if there are unmatched criteria
154	    if unmatched_criteria:
155	        logging.warning(f"ID: {task_id} - {len(unmatched_criteria)} criteria without exact matches: {unmatched_criteria}")
156	    
157	    results["target"]["total"] = total_target_score
158	    results["reference"]["total"] = total_reference_score
159	
160	    return results

Ensure your output follows the specified `<output_format>` and that the JSON format is parsable, with all characters that might cause JSON parsing errors properly escaped.

Ensure your output follows the specified `<output_format>` and that the JSON format is parsable, with all characters that might cause JSON parsing errors properly escaped.

1	import json
2	import os
3	import threading
4	import concurrent.futures
5	import argparse
6	from tqdm import tqdm
7	import logging
8	import time
9	import re 
10	from utils.api import AIClient
11	from utils.io_utils import load_jsonl
12	import glob
13	
14	# Import scoring prompts for Chinese and English
15	from prompt.score_prompt_zh import generate_merged_score_prompt as zh_merged_score_prompt
16	from prompt.score_prompt_en import generate_merged_score_prompt as en_merged_score_prompt
17	from utils.score_calculator import calculate_weighted_scores
18	from utils.json_extractor import extract_json_from_markdown
19	from utils.clean_article import ArticleCleaner
20	
21	# Configure logging - 将级别从INFO改为WARNING
22	logging.basicConfig(level=logging.WARNING, format='%(asctime)s - %(levelname)s - %(message)s')
23	
24	# 关键信息仍然需要输出，设置当前模块的日志级别为INFO
25	logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)
26	logger.setLevel(logging.INFO)
27	
28	# Fixed configuration parameters
29	CRITERIA_FILE = "data/criteria_data/criteria.jsonl"
30	REFERENCE_FILE = "data/test_data/cleaned_data/reference.jsonl"
31	MAX_RETRIES = 10
32	
33	def format_criteria_list(criteria_data):
34	    """Format evaluation criteria list as JSON string, without weight information"""
35	    criteria_for_prompt = {}
36	    criterions_dict = criteria_data.get("criterions", {})
37	
38	    for dim, criterions_list in criterions_dict.items():
39	        if not isinstance(criterions_list, list):
40	            logger.warning(f"Value for dimension '{dim}' is not a list. Skipping.")
41	            continue
42	
43	        criteria_for_prompt[dim] = []
44	        for crit_item in criterions_list:
45	            if isinstance(crit_item, dict) and "criterion" in crit_item and "explanation" in crit_item:
46	                criteria_for_prompt[dim].append({
47	                    "criterion": crit_item["criterion"],
48	                    "explanation": crit_item["explanation"]
49	                })
50	            else:
51	                logger.warning(f"Invalid criteria format in dimension '{dim}'. Skipping item.")
52	
53	    try:
54	        return json.dumps(criteria_for_prompt, ensure_ascii=False, indent=2)
55	    except TypeError as e:
56	        raise ValueError(f"Failed to serialize criteria to JSON: {e}")
57	
58	def process_single_item(task_data, target_articles_map, reference_articles_map, criteria_map, 
59	                         llm_client, lock, pbar, max_retries, language):
60	    """Process a single task: get data, call LLM, parse results, calculate scores"""
61	    task_id = task_data.get('id')
62	    prompt = task_data.get('prompt')
63	
64	    # Data retrieval and validation
65	    if prompt not in target_articles_map:
66	        logger.error(f"Target article not found for ID {task_id}")
67	        with lock: pbar.update(1)
68	        return {"id": task_id, "prompt": prompt, "error": "Target article not found"}
69	    
70	    if prompt not in reference_articles_map:
71	        logger.error(f"Reference article not found for ID {task_id}")
72	        with lock: pbar.update(1)
73	        return {"id": task_id, "prompt": prompt, "error": "Reference article not found"}
74	    
75	    if prompt not in criteria_map:
76	        logger.error(f"Evaluation criteria not found for ID {task_id}")
77	        with lock: pbar.update(1)
78	        return {"id": task_id, "prompt": prompt, "error": "Evaluation criteria not found"}
79	
80	    target_article_data = target_articles_map[prompt]
81	    reference_article_data = reference_articles_map[prompt]
82	    criteria_data = criteria_map[prompt]
83	
84	    target_article = target_article_data.get('article', '')
85	    reference_article = reference_article_data.get('article', '')
86	
87	    # Format evaluation criteria list in JSON
88	    try:
89	        criteria_list_str = format_criteria_list(criteria_data)
90	    except ValueError as e:
91	        logger.error(f"ID {task_id}: {str(e)}")
92	        with lock: pbar.update(1)
93	        return {"id": task_id, "prompt": prompt, "error": f"Failed to format criteria: {str(e)}"}
94	
95	    # Choose scoring prompt based on language
96	    merged_score_prompt = zh_merged_score_prompt if language == "zh" else en_merged_score_prompt
97	    
98	    # Prepare LLM prompt
99	    user_prompt = merged_score_prompt.format(
100	        task_prompt=prompt,
101	        article_1=target_article,
102	        article_2=reference_article,
103	        criteria_list=criteria_list_str 
104	    )
105	
106	    llm_response_str = None
107	    llm_output_json = None
108	    success = False
109	    retry_count = 0
110	
111	    while retry_count < max_retries and not success:
112	        try:
113	            llm_response_str = llm_client.generate(
114	                user_prompt=user_prompt,
115	                system_prompt=""
116	            )
117	
118	            # Extract JSON from response
119	            json_str_extracted = extract_json_from_markdown(llm_response_str)
120	            if not json_str_extracted:
121	                raise ValueError("Failed to extract JSON from LLM response")
122	                
123	            llm_output_json = json.loads(json_str_extracted)
124	            
125	            # Check if all required dimensions exist
126	            expected_dims = ["comprehensiveness", "insight", "instruction_following", "readability"]
127	            if not all(dim in llm_output_json for dim in expected_dims):
128	                missing_dims = [dim for dim in expected_dims if dim not in llm_output_json]
129	                raise ValueError(f"Missing expected dimensions: {missing_dims}")
130	            
131	            # All checks passed
132	            success = True
133	            
134	        except Exception as e:
135	            retry_count += 1
136	            if retry_count < max_retries:
137	                logger.warning(f"ID {task_id}: Retry {retry_count}/{max_retries} - {str(e)}")
138	                time.sleep(1.5 ** retry_count)
139	            else:
140	                logger.error(f"ID {task_id}: Failed after {max_retries} retries - {str(e)}")
141	    
142	    if not success:
143	        with lock: pbar.update(1)
144	        return {
145	            "id": task_id,
146	            "prompt": prompt,
147	            "error": f"Failed to get valid response after {max_retries} retries",
148	            "model_output": llm_response_str[:500] if llm_response_str else "No response"
149	        }
150	
151	    # Calculate weighted scores
152	    try:
153	        scores = calculate_weighted_scores(llm_output_json, criteria_data, language)
154	        
155	        # Calculate overall score = target / (target + reference)
156	        target_total = scores["target"]["total"]
157	        reference_total = scores["reference"]["total"]
158	        overall_score = 0
159	        if target_total + reference_total > 0:
160	            overall_score = target_total / (target_total + reference_total)
161	        
162	        # Calculate normalized dimension scores
163	        normalized_dims = {}
164	        for dim in ["comprehensiveness", "insight", "instruction_following", "readability"]:
165	            dim_key = f"{dim}_weighted_avg"
166	            if dim_key in scores["target"]["dims"]:
167	                target_score = scores["target"]["dims"][dim_key]
168	                reference_score = scores["reference"]["dims"][dim_key]
169	                if target_score + reference_score > 0:
170	                    normalized_dims[dim] = target_score / (target_score + reference_score)
171	                else:
172	                    normalized_dims[dim] = 0
173	            else:
174	                logger.warning(f"ID {task_id}: Missing dimension {dim_key} in scores")
175	                normalized_dims[dim] = 0
176	        
177	    except Exception as e:
178	        logger.error(f"ID {task_id}: Error calculating scores - {str(e)}")
179	        with lock: pbar.update(1)
180	        return {
181	            "id": task_id,
182	            "prompt": prompt,
183	            "error": f"Error calculating scores: {str(e)}"
184	        }
185	
186	    # Prepare final result with simplified format
187	    final_result = {
188	        "id": task_id,
189	        "prompt": prompt,
190	        "comprehensiveness": normalized_dims.get("comprehensiveness", 0),
191	        "insight": normalized_dims.get("insight", 0),
192	        "instruction_following": normalized_dims.get("instruction_following", 0),
193	        "readability": normalized_dims.get("readability", 0),
194	        "overall_score": overall_score
195	    }
196	
197	    with lock:
198	        pbar.update(1)
199	
200	    return final_result
201	
202	def process_language_data(language, target_model, llm_client, clean_agent, 
203	                         raw_data_dir, cleaned_data_dir, max_workers, limit, query_file):
204	    """Process data for a single language (Chinese or English)"""
205	    
206	    # Step 1: Clean target model articles if needed
207	    logger.info(f"Checking if {target_model} articles need cleaning...")
208	    try:
209	        article_cleaner = ArticleCleaner(clean_agent)
210	        default_language = language
211	
212	        article_cleaner.clean_articles(
213	            target_model, 
214	            raw_data_dir, 
215	            cleaned_data_dir, 
216	            max_workers,
217	            MAX_RETRIES,
218	            limit,
219	            default_language
220	            )
221	        cleaning_success = True
222	    except Exception as e:
223	        logger.error(f"Article cleaning failed for {target_model}: {e}")
224	        cleaning_success = False
225	
226	    
227	    if not cleaning_success:
228	        logger.error(f"Article cleaning failed for {target_model}, cannot continue.")
229	        return None
230	    
231	    # Step 2: Load data for scoring
232	    logger.info(f"Loading {language} data from {query_file}...")
233	    
234	    try:
235	        all_tasks = load_jsonl(query_file)
236	        all_tasks = [task for task in all_tasks if task.get('language') == language]
237	            
238	        # Apply limit if specified
239	        if limit is not None and limit > 0:
240	            all_tasks = all_tasks[:limit]
241	            
242	        # Get prompts from tasks
243	        task_prompts = {task['prompt'] for task in all_tasks if 'prompt' in task}
244	        
245	        # Load criteria data
246	        all_criteria = load_jsonl(CRITERIA_FILE)
247	        criteria_list = [c for c in all_criteria if c.get('prompt') in task_prompts]
248	            
249	        # Load target model articles
250	        target_file = os.path.join(cleaned_data_dir, f"{target_model}.jsonl")
251	        all_target_articles = load_jsonl(target_file)
252	        target_articles_list = [a for a in all_target_articles if a.get('prompt') in task_prompts]
253	        if not target_articles_list:
254	            logger.error(f"No target articles found for model {target_model} in {language}")
255	            return None
256	            
257	        # Load reference articles
258	        all_reference_articles = load_jsonl(REFERENCE_FILE)
259	        reference_articles_list = [a for a in all_reference_articles if a.get('prompt') in task_prompts]
260	            
261	        # Build mappings
262	        criteria_map = {item['prompt']: item for item in criteria_list}
263	        target_articles_map = {item['prompt']: item for item in target_articles_list}
264	        reference_articles_map = {item['prompt']: item for item in reference_articles_list}
265	        
266	        # Check for missing data
267	        for task in all_tasks:
268	            prompt = task.get('prompt')
269	            if prompt not in criteria_map:
270	                logger.warning(f"No criteria found for task prompt: {prompt[:50]}...")
271	            if prompt not in target_articles_map:
272	                logger.warning(f"No target article found for task prompt: {prompt[:50]}...")
273	            if prompt not in reference_articles_map:
274	                logger.warning(f"No reference article found for task prompt: {prompt[:50]}...")
275	                
276	        # Filter out tasks with missing data
277	        tasks_to_process = [task for task in all_tasks 
278	                           if task.get('prompt') in criteria_map
279	                           and task.get('prompt') in target_articles_map
280	                           and task.get('prompt') in reference_articles_map]
281	        
282	        if not tasks_to_process:
283	            logger.error(f"No complete task data found for {language}")
284	            return None
285	            
286	        logger.info(f"Processing {len(tasks_to_process)} {language} tasks...")
287	        
288	    except Exception as e:
289	        logger.error(f"Error loading data: {str(e)}")
290	        return None
291	    
292	    # Step 3: Process each task and generate scores
293	    lock = threading.Lock()
294	    results_list = []
295	    
296	    with tqdm(total=len(tasks_to_process), desc=f"Scoring {language} {target_model}") as pbar:
297	        with concurrent.futures.ThreadPoolExecutor(max_workers=max_workers) as executor:
298	            futures = [
299	                executor.submit(
300	                    process_single_item,
301	                    task,
302	                    target_articles_map,
303	                    reference_articles_map,
304	                    criteria_map,
305	                    llm_client,
306	                    lock,
307	                    pbar,
308	                    MAX_RETRIES,
309	                    language
310	                )
311	                for task in tasks_to_process
312	            ]
313	            
314	            for future in concurrent.futures.as_completed(futures):
315	                result = future.result()
316	                if result:
317	                    results_list.append(result)
318	    
319	    successful_results = [res for res in results_list if "error" not in res]
320	    
321	    logger.info(f"{language} evaluation complete. Successfully scored {len(successful_results)} "
322	                 f"out of {len(tasks_to_process)} tasks.")
323	    
324	    return successful_results
325	
326	def main():
327	    parser = argparse.ArgumentParser(description='Score model articles against reference articles using detailed evaluation criteria and LLM.')
328	    parser.add_argument('target_model', type=str, help='Name of target model to evaluate')
329	    parser.add_argument('--limit', type=int, default=None, help='Limit on number of prompts to process (for testing).')
330	    parser.add_argument('--skip_cleaning', action='store_true', help='Skip article cleaning step.')
331	    parser.add_argument('--only_zh', action='store_true', help='Only process Chinese data.')
332	    parser.add_argument('--only_en', action='store_true', help='Only process English data.')
333	    parser.add_argument('--force', action='store_true', help='Force re-evaluation even if results exist.')
334	    
335	    # Add only the parameters that need to be configurable via command line
336	    parser.add_argument('--raw_data_dir', type=str, default="data/test_data/raw_data", help='Directory containing raw data.')
337	    parser.add_argument('--cleaned_data_dir', type=str, default="data/test_data/cleaned_data", help='Directory for cleaned data.')
338	    parser.add_argument('--max_workers', type=int, default=5, help='Maximum number of worker threads.')
339	    parser.add_argument('--query_file', type=str, default="data/prompt_data/query.jsonl", help='Path to query file with language information.')
340	    parser.add_argument('--output_dir', type=str, default="results", help='Directory for output results.')
341	
342	    args = parser.parse_args()
343	
344	    # Extract parameters from args
345	    target_model = args.target_model
346	    limit = args.limit
347	    skip_cleaning = args.skip_cleaning
348	    only_zh = args.only_zh
349	    only_en = args.only_en
350	    force = args.force
351	    raw_data_dir = args.raw_data_dir
352	    cleaned_data_dir = args.cleaned_data_dir
353	    max_workers = args.max_workers
354	    query_file = args.query_file
355	    output_dir = args.output_dir
356	    
357	    os.makedirs(output_dir, exist_ok=True)
358	    
359	    # check if the results file exists
360	    output_file = os.path.join(output_dir, "raw_results.jsonl")
361	    result_file = os.path.join(output_dir, "race_result.txt")
362	    existing_results = []
363	    existing_ids = set()
364	    
365	    if os.path.exists(output_file) and not force:
366	        try:
367	            existing_results = load_jsonl(output_file)
368	            existing_ids = {r.get('id') for r in existing_results if r.get('id')}
369	            logger.info(f"Found existing results file with {len(existing_results)} entries")
370

import json,glob,os
import statistics as st
crit={ }

    r=json.loads(l); crit[r['id']]=r['dimension_weight']
dims=['comprehensiveness','insight','instruction_following','readability']

# Validate identity: odds(Overall) = sum_d w_d * odds_d   (exact iff reference raw r_d constant across d)
files=sorted(glob.glob('results/race/*/raw_results.jsonl'))
print('%-38s %5s %7s %7s %7s'%('model','n','actual','pred','err'))

    if not rows: continue
    pa=[];pp=[]

        K=0.0; ok=True
        for d in dims:

    if not pa: continue
    a=st.mean(pa)*100; p=st.mean(pp)*100

print()

Based on the search results, here's what I found regarding position bias in LLM-as-a-judge evaluation with the MT-Bench framework:

## Position Bias Magnitude

Position bias was measured at 10 to 15 points of winrate swing depending on slot order in the MT-Bench paper. More specifically, in pairwise comparison, the response in slot A wins more often than chance, with 10 to 15 points of winrate swing on close calls being the standard number from Zheng et al.

## Consistency and Swap Behavior

Position bias is the tendency to favour whichever response appears first in a pairwise comparison, and in the original MT-Bench measurements, GPT-4 changed its preferred answer when the order was swapped on roughly a third of cases.

## Mitigation Strategy

The fix is to evaluate every comparison in both orders and only count cases where the verdict is consistent.

## Original Research

The original work by Lianmin Zheng and colleagues examined limitations of LLM-as-a-judge, including position, verbosity, and self-enhancement biases, which formed the basis for understanding these systematic biases in LLM judges.

Based on the search results, I found comprehensive information about AlpacaEval's length bias issue and Dubois et al.'s 2024 solution. Here's what the research reveals:

## The Length Bias Problem

AlpacaEval revealed systematic biases, most notably length bias, which could be exploited by models generating artificially verbose responses to "game" win-rate metrics. Simple, known confounders such as preference for longer outputs remain in existing automated evaluation metrics.

## Length-Controlled AlpacaEval Solution

Dubois et al. (2024) created "Length-controlled alpacaeval: A simple way to debias automatic evaluators". The length-controlled win rate (LC-WR) addresses the observed "length bias"—the tendency of auto-annotators to favor longer responses regardless of their intrinsic quality. The length-controlled win rate is computed by conditioning the underlying preference regression on no difference in output length, yielding a debiased, interpretable estimate of model performance.

## Methodology

The approach uses a simple regression analysis approach for controlling biases in auto-evaluations. The length-controlled win rate uses the official LC protocol, which fits a logistic regression model to separate quality effects from length effects and then evaluates the implied win probability after setting the length-difference feature to zero.

## Spurious Correlations

The search results reference spurious correlations in reference-free evaluation of text generation as a related concern, though the Dubois et al. work specifically focused on the length bias as the primary confounder affecting LLM-based evaluations.

Based on the search results, I found relevant information about the Panickssery 2024 research on LLM evaluators, self-recognition, and self-preference bias:

## Key Findings

Panickssery et al. (2024) showed GPT-4 recognizes its own generations and demonstrated a link between self-recognition and self-preference.

The strength of self-preference bias is linearly correlated with the LLM's self-recognition capability. This was measured across models evaluated on the CNN/Dailymail dataset.

## Definition of Self-Preference Bias

Self-preference is a bias where an LLM evaluator scores its own outputs higher than others' while human annotators consider them of equal quality.

## Research Context

Panickssery et al. [2024a] showed that frontier LLMs both recognize and favor their own outputs, with stronger recognition amplifying bias. Fine-tuning intensified both effects, underscoring risks when the same model serves as generator and judge.

The full paper titled "LLM Evaluators Recognize and Favor Their Own Generations" was published at NeurIPS 2024 and is available on arXiv. The research provides empirical evidence that LLMs can distinguish their own outputs from those of other models and humans, and this recognition ability is correlated with the degree of self-preference bias they exhibit during evaluation tasks.

Based on the search results, here's what research has found about formatting bias and style-controlled evaluations in Chatbot Arena:

## The Problem: Markdown Formatting Bias

Pairwise voting reliably rewards longer responses with bullet points and markdown formatting, even when content quality is similar. Responses with more formatting (markdown headers, bullet points) tend to win, and models that use more hedging and caveats may be penalized even when more accurate.

This created a significant distortion: Some models had developed long-form, structured response styles partly because they helped on Arena, which arguably distorted the leaderboard relative to pure content-quality preference.

## The Mitigation: Style-Controlled Elo

In November 2024 the LMArena team introduced style-controlled Elo, which attempts to remove the effect of formatting and verbosity from the headline number. However, style-controlled ratings attempt to mitigate these biases but do not fully eliminate them.

## Measured Effects on LLM Judges

Research on LLM-as-a-Judge shows significant variation: For four judges that exceed the human markdown preference rate, the gap is large enough that uncorrected formatting differences may dominate genuine quality differences in close model-vs-model comparisons. Notably, Claude and GPT-4o are more quality-sensitive (penalize filler content while still rewarding genuine completeness), compared to other models that show stronger formatting bias.

Human researchers recommend: Evaluation frameworks should either normalize formatting before judging or explicitly instruct judges to disregard it.

## Preference Fairness Findings

Preference Fairness (PF) scores ranged dramatically. GPT-4 maintained "−0.12" (primacy bias) on MTBench, while "Claude-3-Opus" showed "0.22" (recency bias). The authors noted: "a high position consistency does not guarantee fairness."

## Answer Quality Gap Impact

The research confirmed position bias correlates strongly with answer quality differences. The authors stated: "answers with larger quality disparities are easier to achieve judgment consistency, whereas those of similar quality are difficult to judge."

## Single-Answer Evaluation

The study did **not** investigate whether judges score answers separately versus selecting winners—it focused exclusively on comparative judgment paradigms (pairwise and list-wise ranking).

Deep Research Agents are a prominent category of LLM-based agents. By autonomously orchestrating
multistep web exploration, targeted retrieval, and higher-order synthesis, they transform vast amounts of
online information into analyst-grade, citation-rich reports—compressing hours of manual desk research
into minutes. However, a comprehensive benchmark for systematically evaluating the capabilities of these
agents remains absent. To bridge this gap, we present DeepResearch Bench, a benchmark consisting
of 100 PhD-level research tasks, each meticulously crafted by domain experts across 22 distinct fields.
Evaluating DRAs is inherently complex and labor-intensive. We therefore propose two novel methodologies
that achieve strong alignment with human judgment. The first is a reference-based method with adaptive
criteria to assess the quality of generated research reports. The other framework is introduced to evaluate
DRA’s information retrieval and collection capabilities by assessing its effective citation count and overall
citation accuracy. We have open-sourced DeepResearch Bench and key components of these frameworks at
https://github.com/Ayanami0730/deep_research_bench to accelerate the development of practical LLM-
based agents.

# LLM-as-a-Judge Bias Analysis: Exact Figures

## Position Bias (Table 2)

**GPT-4 Consistency:** "65.0%" with default prompt; "66.2%" with rename prompt

**Claude-v1 Consistency:** "23.8%" (default); "56.2%" (rename)

**GPT-3.5 Consistency:** "46.2%" (default); "51.2%" (rename)

**Bias Direction (GPT-4):** "30.0%" favored first position; "5.0%" favored second

## Verbosity Bias (Table 3)

The research team designed a "repetitive list" attack using 23 answers. Failure rates when judges incorrectly rated unnecessarily verbose responses as superior:

- **Claude-v1:** "91.3%" failure rate
- **GPT-3.5:** "91.3%" failure rate  
- **GPT-4:** "8.7%" failure rate

## Self-Enhancement Bias

The paper notes this phenomenon statistically but avoids definitive quantification. Key finding: "GPT-4 favors itself with a 10% higher win rate; Claude-v1 favors itself with a 25% higher win rate." However, the authors acknowledge "our study cannot determine whether the models exhibit a self-enhancement bias" due to limited controlled data.

## Summary

GPT-4 demonstrated substantially greater robustness across all three bias categories compared to competing models, though position bias remained measurable even for the strongest judge.

Length-Controlled AlpacaEval:
A Simple Way to Debias Automatic Evaluators
Yann Dubois1, Balázs Galambosi2, Percy Liang1 and Tatsunori B. Hashimoto1
1Stanford University 2Independent Researcher
Abstract
LLM-based auto-annotators have become a key component of the LLM de-
velopment process due to their cost-effectiveness and scalability compared
to human-based evaluation. However, these auto-annotators can introduce
biases that are hard to remove. Even simple, known confounders such
as preference for longer outputs remain in existing automated evaluation
metrics. We propose a simple regression analysis approach for controlling
biases in auto-evaluations. As a real case study, we focus on reducing the
length bias of AlpacaEval, a fast and affordable benchmark for instruction-
tuned LLMs that uses LLMs to estimate response quality. Despite being
highly correlated with human preferences, AlpacaEval is known to favor
models that generate longer outputs. We introduce a length-controlled
AlpacaEval that aims to answer the counterfactual question: "What would
the preference be if the model’s and baseline’s output had the same
length?" To achieve this, we first fit a generalized linear model to predict
the biased auto-annotator’s preferences based on the mediators we want to
control for (length difference) and other relevant features. We then obtain
length-controlled preferences by predicting preferences while conditioning
the GLM with a zero difference in lengths. Length-controlling not only
improves the robustness of the metric to manipulations in model verbosity,
we also find that it increases the Spearman correlation with LMSYS Chatbot
Arena from 0.94 to 0.98. We release the code and resulting leaderboard.
1 Introduction
Figure 1: AlpacaEval length-controlled increases correlation with the LMSYS Chatbot Arena
from 0.94 to 0.98. It is currently the automatic benchmark with the highest correlation with
Chatbot Arena.

quantify progress. In closed-ended tasks, such as multiple-choice QA, such evaluations are
straightforward to implement and trust (Novikova et al., 2017; Yeh et al., 2021). However,
such evaluations cannot be applied to open-ended settings such as instruction following
for language models. Even neural reference-based evaluation metrics such as BERTscore
(Zhang* et al., 2020) face challenges in those settings due to the difficulty of collecting a
diverse set of references that can cover the space of valid outputs.
1

annotators, they often do so by exploiting spurious correlations such as the length of the

4.1 AlpacaEval-LC decreases length gameability
A good evaluation metric should not be so sensitive to length that prompting for longer or
shorter responses completely changes the metric. To measure gameability we prompted dif-
ferent models to “Answer with as much detail as possible.” (verbose) or “Be as concise as pos-
sible while still providing all the necessary information to answer the question.” (concise).
Figure 3 shows that AlpacaEval is highly length gameable. The baseline model
(gpt4_1106_preview) fluctuates from 22.9% to 64.3% by varying the verbosity instruc-
tion in the prompt. Even worse, significant gains are possible by asking weaker models to
be verbose, as seen with Claude-2.1.
In contrast, the length-controlled AlpacaEval has significantly lower gameability
(gpt4_1106_preview’s win rates now only fluctuate from 41.9% to 51.6%), and rankings are
generally stable to verbosity prompts. Quantitatively, the normalized standard deviation
across the three verbosity prompts decreases from 25% to 10% from the length control.
4.2 AlpacaEval-LC increases correlation with Chatbot Arena to 0.98
Our prior experiments demonstrate that length control reduces the high sensitivity to length
in AlpacaEval. However, our goal is not simply to make metrics that are less sensitive to
length, but to produce metrics that are overall more representative of human judgments.
Figure 1 shows that controlling for length increased the Spearman correlation with Chat
Arena from 0.94 to 0.98. Of existing benchmarks, this difference is significant enough to
make the length-corrected version of AlpacaEval the metric with the highest correlation
with Chat Arena which we are aware of. Correlations are computed on every benchmark

# LLM Judge Bias Analysis: Key Findings

## (1) Style-Bias Metric Definition and Units

The style-bias score operates on a scale of **[-1, 1]**, computed as:
> "b = P(prefers target) − P(prefers non-target)"

For style bias, the target is the markdown-formatted response. Positive values indicate preference for markdown over plain prose. The metric aggregates across pairs where content is identical but formatting differs.

## (2) Per-Judge Bias Scores (Baseline)

| Judge | Style Bias | Position Bias | Verbosity Bias |
|-------|-----------|---------------|----------------|
| Gemini 2.5 Pro | +0.76 | ≤0.04 | +0.40 |
| Claude Sonnet 4 | +0.68 | ≤0.04 | −0.12 |
| GPT-4o | +0.10 | ≤0.04 | −0.04 |
| Llama 3.3-70B | +0.40 | ≤0.04 | +0.44 |
| Gemini 2.5 Flash | +0.72 | ≤0.04 | +0.24 |

## (3) Human-Validation Study Results

Two human annotators evaluated 30 identical content pairs (markdown vs. prose):
> "Aggregate human preference for the markdown side...was 0.57"

(57% markdown preference). LLM judge markdown preferences on the same pairs:
- Gemini Pro: 97% (+40 pp gap vs. humans)
- Gemini Flash: 90% (+33 pp)
- Claude: 83% (+27 pp)
- Llama: 73% (+17 pp)
- GPT-4o: 53% (−3 pp, aligned with humans)

## (4) Exact Judge Models Used

- Gemini 2.5 Pro (Google, frontier)
- Claude Sonnet 4 (Anthropic, frontier)
- GPT-4o (OpenAI, frontier)
- Gemini 2.5 Flash (Google, mid-tier)
- Llama 3.3-70B (Meta, open-source)

## (5) Bias Magnitude Ranking

Style bias dominates all others:
> "Style bias is the dominant bias (0.10–0.76 position-averaged)"

Ranking: **Style (0.10–0.76) >> Verbosity (−0.12 to +0.44) > Position (≤0.04)**

## (6) Debiasing Strategy Effectiveness (MT-Bench, n=400)

Statistically significant improvements (mixed-effects regression):
- **Claude S8**: +11.5 pp (p<0.0001)
- **Gemini Flash S8**: +7.5 pp (p<0.0001)
- **Claude S5**: +7.3 pp (p=0.0009)
- **Gemini Flash S1**: +4.7 pp (p=0.004)
- **Llama S8**: +4.5 pp (p=0.011)

Best overall: Gemini 2.5 Flash with S8 achieved **71.0% agreement** at ~$0.001/eval.

The arithmetic is complete and validated. Here is what I found.

## A. The scoring math — there is a clean exact identity

`Overall = Sint(tgt)/(Sint(tgt)+Sint(ref))` (paper Eq. 3 = `deepresearch_bench_race.py:160`). Because the reference's raw score `r` turns out to be **flat across dimensions**, this collapses to:

> **odds(Overall) = Σ_d w_d · odds(dim_d)**,  where odds(x) = x/(1−x)

I validated this against all 45 local result files: **mean error 0.037 points**, and 0.00 on the 100-task claude-3-7-sonnet run. It reproduces SOTA (predicts 55.76 vs actual 55.78) and the reference (50.00 vs 49.98).

**Two corrections to the brief:**
1. **POLARIS on task 72 is 43.13, not 43.82.** The tuple (45.49/42.38/44.09/37.74) is run `rank12_rank10`, id 72, whose actual `overall_score` is 0.4313. The identity predicts 43.16 — the brief's 43.82 is not consistent with its own dimension scores.
2. **Overall is NOT the weighted average of the four dimension scores.** It's a ratio of weighted sums. Averaging the dims will mislead you.

## B. What raw scores win (r = reference raw ≈ 7.0/10; bounded ≤7.52 by SOTA's insight score)

| | REF | POLARIS | SOTA |
|---|---|---|---|
| comp | 7.00 | **5.84** | 9.03 |
| insight | 7.00 | **5.15** | 9.31 |
| IF | 7.00 | **5.52** | 8.66 |
| read | 7.00 | **4.24** | 7.57 |

Recipes (readability lifted by presentation, remainder spread over content dims):
- **→50:** read 8.0, comp 7.19, ins 6.50, IF 6.87  (+1.35 raw on each content dim)
- **→52:** read 8.0, comp 7.87, ins 7.17, IF 7.55  (+2.03)
- **→55:** read 8.0, comp 9.00, ins 8.30, IF 8.68  (+3.16 — i.e. "excellent/outstanding")

## C. Readability is the MOST expensive point on the board, not the cheapest

Raw points needed per **+1.00 overall** (at r=7.0): **insight 0.68 · comp 0.75 · IF 0.87 · readability 1.55.**

The 0.14 weight makes readability **2.3× more expensive per raw point than insight**. And the hard result:

> **A perfect 10/10 readability cannot reach parity.** Maxing readability alone takes POLARIS to **46.65** (46.3–47.1 across r=6.5–7.5). It is mathematically capped below 50.

Decomposing the 12.65-point gap to SOTA in ΔK: **insight 37.9%, comprehensiveness 26.3%, IF 22.4%, readability 13.3%.** SOTA did not get there on presentation — its readability raw (7.57) barely beats the reference; it wins on insight (9.31).

## D. Judge biases — verified against primary sources

The decisive finding: **the RACE judge is Gemini-2.5-Pro** (paper §4, line 166), and in a controlled 2026 study Gemini 2.5 Pro has **the worst style bias of any judge tested: +0.76 on [−1,1]** — it prefers markdown-formatted text **97% of the time when the content is byte-identical**, versus **57% for humans** (a +40pp bias gap). Style bias ≫ verbosity (+0.40) ≫ position (≤0.04).

- **Length/verbosity (documented, large):** AlpacaEval — gpt-4-1106's win rate swings **22.9% → 64.3%** from a verbosity instruction alone, no model change. Length control cuts normalized σ from 25%→10% and raises Arena Spearman 0.94→0.98.
- **Structure/markdown (documented, largest, and applies directly):** LMArena style control coefficients — length 0.249, list 0.031, header 0.024, bold 0.019.
- **Position (documented but NOT your lever):** MT-Bench GPT-4 consistency 65.0% (30.0% first vs 5.0% second) — but that's winner-picking. RACE scores both articles separately, and its own **Reverse Position ablation moves PAR only 71.33→69.56**. ≤0.04 magnitude in the rubric setting.
- **Self-preference (structural, unfixable by us):** judge = Gemini-2.5-Pro, reference = Gemini-2.5-Pro Deep Research. Panickssery et al. (NeurIPS 2024) link self-recognition to self-preference. The reference scoring ~50 against itself is close to a **tautology** and should not be read as evidence the judge is neutral.

## E. How much of the 12-point gap is presentation?

**Our articles have zero tables, zero bold, and (in the best runs) zero bullets.** Task-72 reference: 9029 words, 33 headings, 91 bullets, 18 table rows, 119 bold. 99% of reference articles contain tables. The readability rubric explicitly scores "Information Presentation and Visual Elements... charts or other visual elements."

- **Floor (solid):** fixing readability 4.24→8.0 by presentation alone = **+2.36 pts (43.13 → 45.49)**.

findings: EXACT SCORING IDENTITY DERIVED AND VALIDATED: odds(Overall) = SUM_d w_d * odds(dim_d), where odds(x)=x/(1-x). This follows from Eq.3 of the paper / deepresearch_bench_race.py:160 (Overall = target/(target+reference)) PLUS the empirical fact that the reference's raw score r is flat across dimensions. Validated against all 45 local raw_results.jsonl files: mean absolute error 0.037 points; error 0.00 on the 100-task claude-3-7-sonnet run. It reproduces SOTA (predicts 55.76 vs actual 55.78) and the reference (predicts 50.00 vs actual 49.98).

CORRECTION TO THE BRIEF #2: Overall is NOT a weighted average of the four reported dimension scores. It is a RATIO OF WEIGHTED SUMS (weighted-sum of target raws over weighted-sum of target+reference raws). The four reported dimension numbers are themselves per-dimension ratios. Averaging them with the dimension weights gives the wrong answer. Only the odds are additive.

THE HEADLINE ARITHMETIC RESULT — READABILITY IS THE MOST EXPENSIVE POINT ON THE BOARD, NOT THE CHEAPEST. Raw points required per +1.00 overall point (at r=7.0): insight 0.68, comprehensiveness 0.75, instruction-following 0.87, readability 1.55. The 0.14 weight makes readability 2.3x MORE expensive per raw point than insight. The user's hypothesis is arithmetically backwards.

IMPLIED RAW SCORES (r=7.0, bounded <=7.52 by SOTA's insight odds): REFERENCE 7.00 on every dim; POLARIS comp 5.84 / insight 5.15 / IF 5.52 / readability 4.24; SOTA comp 9.03 / insight 9.31 / IF 8.66 / readability 7.57. DECISIVE BIAS FINDING — THE JUDGE IS THE WORST-BIASED JUDGE FOR EXACTLY OUR WEAKNESS. RACE's judge is Gemini-2.5-Pro (paper sec.4, 'we utilize Gemini-2.5-pro as the Judge LLM'). In a controlled study (arXiv 2604.23178, 'Judging the Judges'), Gemini 2.5 Pro shows the LARGEST style bias of any judge tested: +0.76 on a [-1,1] scale, preferring markdown-formatted responses 97% of the time when the content is BYTE-IDENTICAL, versus 57% for human annotators (a +40 percentage-point judge-vs-human gap). Bias ranking for that judge: style (+0.76) >> verbosity (+0.40) >> position (<=0.04).

SELF-PREFERENCE IS STRUCTURAL AND UNFIXABLE BY US: the judge is Gemini-2.5-Pro and the reference articles are Gemini-2.5-Pro Deep Research outputs (paper: 'reference reports used in RACE's scoring were selected from deep research articles generated by the Gemini-2.5-pro-based Deep Research'). Panickssery et al. (NeurIPS 2024) establish a linear correlation between self-recognition and self-preference. CONSEQUENCE: the reference scoring ~49.98 'against itself' is close to a TAUTOLOGY and is NOT evidence that the judge is neutral. Every target is being graded against the judge's own house style by the judge.

POSITION BIAS IS NOT THE LEVER (despite being the most-cited bias). MT-Bench (Zheng et al., verified from PDF Table 2): GPT-4 consistency 65.0%, biased-toward-first 30.0% vs biased-toward-second 5.0% — but that is WINNER-PICKING. RACE scores both articles separately against rubric criteria, which largely dissolves position bias: 'Judging the Judges' measures <=0.04 for ALL judges in the rubric setting, and RACE's OWN 'Reverse Position' ablation (paper Table 2) moves Pairwise Agreement Rate only 71.33 -> 69.56. Note also that RACE always places the target as article_1 (deepresearch_bench_race.py:101-103) with no swap, so whatever small primacy effect exists applies uniformly to every model on the board and is not a differentiator.

LENGTH/VERBOSITY BIAS IS DOCUMENTED AND LARGE (verified from the Dubois et al. PDF): on AlpacaEval, gpt-4-1106-preview's win rate swings from 22.9% to 64.3% purely from a verbosity instruction, with NO change to the underlying model. Length control cuts the normalized standard deviation across verbosity prompts from 25% to 10% and raises Spearman correlation with Chatbot Arena from 0.94 to 0.98. Gemini 2.5 Pro's own measured verbosity bias is +0.40.

STRUCTURE/MARKDOWN BIAS QUANTIFIED (LMArena style control, verified): normalized Bradley-Terry style coefficients are length 0.249, markdown list 0.031, markdown header 0.024, markdown bold 0.019 — 'length was the dominant style factor; all other markdown effects are second order.' Controlling style moved real models by 5-12 rank positions (GPT-4o-mini 6->11, Grok-2-mini 6->18, Claude 3 Opus 16->10). NOTE: those coefficients are for HUMAN voters; for the Gemini LLM judge the markdown effect is far larger (+0.76).

MEASURED PRESENTATION DEFICIT IN OUR OWN ARTICLES: every POLARIS task-72 run has ZERO tables and ZERO bold; the best runs have ZERO bullets. rank12_rank10 = 7692 words / 12 headings / 0 bullets / 0 table rows / 0 bold. The task-72 REFERENCE = 9029 words / 33 headings / 91 bullets / 18 table rows / 119 bold. Across the reference corpus: mean 5137 words, 21.3 headings, 100.4 bullets, and 99% of the 100 reference articles contain tables. The readability rubric explicitly scores 'Information Presentation and Visual Elements ... appropriately uses charts or other visual elements'. We are scoring a near-zero on a criterion we are simply not attempting.

ANSWER TO 'HOW MUCH OF THE 12-POINT GAP IS PRESENTATION': FLOOR (solid) = +2.36 points. Fixing readability from 4.24 to 8.0 raw by presentation alone moves 43.13 -> 45.49. CEILING (speculative) = ~48.9-50.5, IF the +0.76 style bias produces a halo that also lifts the content dimensions by +1.0 to +1.5 raw. So presentation is worth ~2.4 points guaranteed and up to ~7 if the halo is real. The halo is the load-bearing untested assumption. Content remains the majority of the gap under every assumption.

RECOMMENDED NEXT EXPERIMENT (clean, one variable): take an existing POLARIS article, reformat it with headings/bullets/tables/bold WITHOUT changing a single claim, and re-score. This isolates the style-bias halo and directly measures how much of the gap is presentation. Given Gemini-2.5-Pro's 97%-vs-57% markdown preference on identical content, this is the highest-information-per-dollar experiment available.
